perturbation.original_text: Within 30 days after the end of each month during the Term, CNET will provide a report to the Company indicating the number of Retail Promotions displayed on the CNET Sites during such month and the number of times that a User clicked on a Retail Promotion during such month.
perturbation.changed_text: Within 60 days after the end of each month during the Term, CNET will provide a report to the Company indicating the number of Retail Promotions displayed on the CNET Sites during such month and the number of times that a User clicked on a Retail Promotion during such month.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the reporting deadline from 30 to 60 days after the end of the month could be problematic if California law mandates a shorter deadline for providing certain information relevant to the promotion and sales data. While this contract doesn't explicitly detail the type of report, if it encompasses sales data or information relevant to commissions, a 30-day reporting period might be implicitly required by labor laws, and extending it to 60 days introduces a contradiction.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Labor Code § 204 mandates the timely payment of wages, which can include commissions or bonuses tied to sales or promotional performance. If the 'Retail Promotions' directly influence employee compensation (e.g., commissions based on clicks or sales generated), delaying the reporting on these promotions to 60 days could indirectly violate the requirement for timely wage payment, as it delays the calculation and disbursement of any related compensation. Thus, while the contract deals with business to business promotions, any variable compensation would be subject to wage requirements.

EMPLOYMENT REGULATION AND SUPERVISION [200 - 2699.8] ( Division 2 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) PART 1. COMPENSATION [200 - 452] ( Part 1 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) CHAPTER 1. Payment of Wages [200 - 273] ( Chapter 1 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) ARTICLE 1. General Occupations [200 - 244] ( Article 1 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) 204. (a) All wages, other than those mentioned in Section 201, 201.3, 202, 204.1, or 204.2, earned by any person in any employment are due and payable twice during each calendar month, on days designated in advance by the employer as the regular paydays. Labor performed between the 1st and 15th days, inclusive, of any calendar month shall be paid for between the 16th and the 26th day of the month during which the labor was performed, and labor performed between the 16th and the last day, inclusive, of any calendar month, shall be paid for between the 1st and 10th day of the following month. However, salaries of executive, administrative, and professional employees of employers covered by the Fair Labor Standards Act, as set forth pursuant to Section 13(a)(1) of the Fair Labor Standards Act, as amended through March 1, 1969, in Part 541 of Title 29 of the Code of Federal Regulations, as that pa
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the reporting deadline from 30 to 60 days, which contradicts California Labor Code § 204 if the report contains information relevant to calculating wages, including commissions or bonuses. The law snippet 1 states that wages are due and payable twice during each calendar month, and labor performed between certain days of the month shall be paid for by a specific date the following month. By delaying the report which contributes to calculating those wages, it delays the 'timely payment of wages,' which the law addresses.

perturbation.original_text: Within 30 days after the end of each month during the Term, the Company will provide a report to CNET indicating the aggregate number of referrals from the CNET Sites to the Company Site during such month, the resulting number of buyers, the aggregate behavior (including orders and sales volume) of those buyers, and the total revenue attributable to the Online Promotions minus applicable sales tax, shipping costs, returns and cancellations (the "CNET Sales").
perturbation.changed_text: Within 45 days after the end of each quarter during the Term, the Company will provide a report to CNET indicating the aggregate number of referrals from the CNET Sites to the Company Site during such month, the resulting number of buyers, the aggregate behavior (including orders and sales volume) of those buyers, and the total revenue attributable to the Online Promotions minus applicable sales tax, shipping costs, returns and cancellations (the "CNET Sales").
perturbation.explanation: Changing the reporting frequency from monthly to quarterly and extending the deadline to 45 days after the end of the quarter introduces a potential conflict. While the contract is B2B, if this 'CNET Sales' data directly impacts commission calculations for California-based employees, this longer reporting period could violate California's labor laws requiring timely wage payment.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Labor Code Section 204 necessitates the prompt payment of wages. Switching from monthly to quarterly reports on 'CNET Sales,' which influences commission calculations, could breach this requirement. If commissions are earned based on these sales, delaying the reporting and subsequent payment beyond the legally permissible timeframe constitutes a violation of California's wage laws. Even in a B2B agreement, if individual compensation is impacted, wage laws prevail.

perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the reporting frequency from monthly to quarterly, and extends the deadline for reporting. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both suggest that this could violate California labor law if the 'CNET Sales' data impacts commission calculations for California-based employees, because California Labor Code Section 204 requires timely wage payment. Scraped Law Snippet 1, from California Code, LAB 204, states that 'All wages... are due and payable twice during each calendar month.' If commissions are considered wages, and their calculation is dependent on the 'CNET Sales' data, then delaying the reporting of this data from monthly to quarterly directly contradicts the legal requirement for timely wage payment.

perturbation.original_text: Payments under this paragraph will be based on the reports prepared by the Company under Section 3.2 (although CNET may challenge such reports as contemplated by Section 9.5) and will be due within 30 days after the end of each month of the Term.
perturbation.changed_text: Payments under this paragraph will be based on the reports prepared by the Company under Section 3.2 (although CNET may challenge such reports as contemplated by Section 9.5) and will be due within 45 days after the end of each quarter of the Term.
perturbation.explanation: Changing payment from monthly to quarterly and extending the deadline from 30 days to 45 days after the quarter's end creates a conflict. If these payments relate to commissions or other forms of employee compensation, this longer period could violate California labor laws mandating the timely payment of wages.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Labor Code § 204 requires employers to pay wages at least twice during each calendar month. By modifying the payment schedule from monthly to quarterly with a 45-day delay, this clause directly contradicts the requirement for timely and frequent wage payments, particularly if the 'Payments' mentioned here encompass commissions, bonuses, or other forms of compensation for services rendered by individuals. While seemingly a B2B agreement, the downstream effects on individuals are key here.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract text changes the payment schedule from monthly to quarterly and extends the payment deadline from 30 to 45 days after the quarter's end. According to California Labor Code § 204, as evidenced by Scraped Law Snippet 1, wages earned by an employee are due and payable twice during each calendar month. The modification to a quarterly payment schedule directly contradicts this legal requirement for timely wage payments, particularly if the payments encompass commissions or bonuses, thus creating a legal contradiction as it violates the state law requirements for payment frequency.
